title: neither gcc nor clang are compliant with standard c++
excerpt: in c++, function types have a "language linkage" associated with them: this is either "C++", "C", or some other implementation-defined language. and the standard very explicitly states that Two function types with different language linkages are distinct types even if they are otherwise identical. the idea is that some implementations may have different calling conventions for c++ functions than for c functions, so they can't be intermixed
content_html:
in c++, function types have a "language linkage" associated with them: this is either "C++", "C", or some other implementation-defined language. and the standard very explicitly states that Two function types with different language linkages are distinct types even if they are otherwise identical. the idea is that some implementations may have different calling conventions for c++ functions than for c functions, so they can't be intermixed

gcc and clang however, just, don't store language linkage information with the type. so two functions with different language linkages can be identical: 

#include <type_traits>
extern "C" using c_func = void ();
// this static assertion should fail, but it doesn't
static_assert(std::is_same<c_func, void ()>::value);

this can also cause erroneous compilation failures when overloading a function which takes in a function pointer parameter: 

extern "C" using c_func = void ();
void f(c_func *) {}
void f(void (*)()) {}

the above code should compile, but gcc and clang both think that the parameter lists are identical and thus this violates the one definition rule

IMO the blame here doesn't lie on gcc or clang; it lies on the standard. that is, the standard is wrong and should be updated to make this implementation-defined. gcc and clang can't change their behavior because that would be a breaking ABI change (extern "C" function types in mangled names would be encoded differently). and they have no reason to make this change: the calling conventions for c and c++ are identical on, as far as i can tell, pretty much every platform

sentences.zh: 在 C++ 中，函数类型具有与其关联的“语言链接”：这是“C++”、“C”或某种其他实现定义的语言。

sentences.en: and the standard very explicitly states that Two function types with different language linkages are distinct types even if they are otherwise identical.
sentences.zh: 标准非常明确地指出，具有不同语言链接的两个函数类型是不同的类型，即使它们在其他方面相同。

sentences.en: the idea is that some implementations may have different calling conventions for c++ functions than for c functions, so they can't be intermixed
sentences.zh: 这个想法是，某些实现对 c++ 函数的调用约定可能与对 c 函数的调用约定不同，因此它们不能混合使用

sentences.en: so two functions with different language linkages can be identical:
sentences.zh: 因此具有不同语言链接的两个函数可以是相同的：

blocks.html_en: IMO the blame here doesn't lie on gcc or clang; it lies on the standard. that is, the standard is wrong and should be updated to make this implementation-defined. gcc and clang can't change their behavior because that would be a breaking ABI change (extern "C" function types in mangled names would be encoded differently). and they have no reason to make this change: the calling conventions for c and c++ are identical on, as far as i can tell, pretty much every platform

sentences.zh: IMO 这里的责任并不在于 gcc 或 clang；它取决于标准。

sentences.en: that is, the standard is wrong and should be updated to make this implementation-defined.
sentences.zh: 也就是说，该标准是错误的，应该更新以定义此实现。

sentences.en: gcc and clang can't change their behavior because that would be a breaking ABI change (extern "C" function types in mangled names would be encoded differently).
sentences.zh: gcc 和 clang 无法更改它们的行为，因为这将是一个破坏性的 ABI 更改（损坏名称中的 extern“C”函数类型将以不同的方式编码）。

sentences.en: and they have no reason to make this change: the calling conventions for c and c++ are identical on, as far as i can tell, pretty much every platform
sentences.zh: 他们没有理由进行此更改：据我所知，c 和 c++ 的调用约定在几乎每个平台上都是相同的

content_html_zh:
在 C++ 中，函数类型具有与其关联的“语言链接”：这是“C++”、“C”或某种其他实现定义的语言。 标准非常明确地指出，具有不同语言链接的两个函数类型是不同的类型，即使它们在其他方面相同。 这个想法是，某些实现对 c++ 函数的调用约定可能与对 c 函数的调用约定不同，因此它们不能混合使用

然而，gcc 和 clang 不存储带有类型的语言链接信息。 因此具有不同语言链接的两个函数可以是相同的：

#include <type_traits>
extern "C" using c_func = void ();
// this static assertion should fail, but it doesn't
static_assert(std::is_same<c_func, void ()>::value);

当重载接受函数指针参数的函数时，这也可能导致错误的编译失败：

extern "C" using c_func = void ();
void f(c_func *) {}
void f(void (*)()) {}

上面的代码应该可以编译，但是 gcc 和 clang 都认为参数列表是相同的，因此这违反了单一定义规则

IMO 这里的责任并不在于 gcc 或 clang；它取决于标准。 也就是说，该标准是错误的，应该更新以定义此实现。 gcc 和 clang 无法更改它们的行为，因为这将是一个破坏性的 ABI 更改（损坏名称中的 extern“C”函数类型将以不同的方式编码）。 他们没有理由进行此更改：据我所知，c 和 c++ 的调用约定在几乎每个平台上都是相同的
